text: The surgeon inserts a 12mm optical port into the right upper quadrant, providing visual access to the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the optical port to be parallel to the abdominal wall after visualizing the peritoneum, preparing to insert it further into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon positions three laparoscopic ports: a 10 mm umbilical port and a 5 mm left upper parodactyl port, in addition to the previously mentioned optical port.
The surgeon disconnects the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, beginning opposite and slightly above the level of the incisura angularis, using an energy source.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and continues the resection upwards towards the gastroesophageal junction, carefully manipulating the tissue in this area.
The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to retract the stomach and liver, utilizing the same instrument due to the absence of an additional port.
The surgeon divides all short gastric vessels, exercising caution while dividing the upper short gastric vessels near the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon divides the pancreaticogastric peritoneal reflection and pancreaticogastric attachments to fully mobilize the stomach fundus, ensuring clear separation from the pancreas.
The surgeon continues dissection up to the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the gastroesophageal pad of fat remains intact to preserve blood supply to the upper part of the sleeve.
The surgeon carefully divides the tissue between the stomach and pancreas, taking caution to avoid the left gastric vessels and splenic artery that may be encountered in this area.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the left crus of the diaphragm is visually identified, ensuring clear exposure of the anatomical landmark.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pylorus, progressing along the stomach while maintaining careful technique to avoid damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon dissects towards the pylorus, carefully avoiding the greater curvature to prevent damage to the sleeve. The greater omentum is disconnected from the greater curvature of the stomach, progressing towards the pylorus.
The surgeon relieves all pancreatic-gastric attachments to fully mobilize the pyloric antrum, completing the devascularization of the greater curvature of the stomach.

The surgeon begins forming the gastric sleeve, starting six centimeters proximal to the pylorus. A six-centimeter width of the pyloric antrum is prepared to facilitate the upcoming horizontal gastro-ileal anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a sixty green stapler to make the initial cut on the stomach, followed by the insertion of a gastric calibration tube to ensure proper sizing and alignment.

The surgeon uses a purple-sixty tri stapler to continue the stapling process, with the right hand instrument retracting the lever as the stapler is fired. Only three ports are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses tri staplers to compress and staple the tissue in increments of two millimeters, pausing for two seconds between each firing to effectively displace tissue fluid and enhance compression.

The surgeon staples the upper part of the stomach, ensuring complete removal of the fundus while maintaining a distance of at least one centimeter from the gastroesophageal junction. Minimal bleeding is observed from the staple edge using this method.
The surgeon applies an additional stapler to the upper part of the stomach to ensure complete resection, addressing any remaining tissue left over from previous stapling.
The divided stomach is positioned in the right upper quadrant, anterior to the liver. An anchoring stitch is placed near the pylorus using a 3-0 PDS suture.

The surgeon divides the omentum into two halves at the site of the proposed anastomosis to reduce tension. The omentum is disconnected from the transverse colon over a length of a few inches, allowing the anastomotic loop to lie comfortably.

The surgeon identifies the ileocecal junction and measures the terminal ileum backwards over a length of 250 to 300 centimeters to determine the point of anastomosis. A storage bowl grasper is used for bubble measurement, with the distance from the tip to the proximal end of printed letters on the shaft being five centimeters, aiding in precise measurement.
The surgeon measures the terminal ileal segment by pushing the measured bubble into the right lower quadrant, using a three hundred centimeter length, requiring sixty counts for accurate measurement.

The surgeon uses a bubble grasper with a ratchet through the right upper quadrant port to measure the ileal loop. A 3-0 PDS suture is used to take a bite on the ileal loop and apply a knot, approximating the pylorus and ileal loop for anastomosis, which is performed in two layers.
The surgeon prepares the anastomosis by creating two layers: an outer seromuscular layer and an inner full thickness layer, ensuring secure tissue approximation.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior seromuscular layer, starting at the pylorus and moving towards the staple line. The anteroinferior aspect of the pyloric antrum is approximated to the ileal loop near the mesenteric border, with periodic tightening of the suture to ensure effective approximation.
The surgeon applies a knot at the left end to secure the posterior seromuscular layer, using the same suture material to continue with the anterior seromuscular layer in the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel or ligature Maryland to create an opening in the gastric sleeve and ileal loop. A calibration tube is inserted into the gastric sleeve, and the opening is made against the tube to ensure precise sizing.

The surgeon creates a gastrotomy over a length of 3 to 4.5 centimeters on the gastric sleeve, avoiding the use of monopolar cautery to prevent separation of the mucosa from the seromuscular layer and reduce bleeding risk.

The ileum is opened along the anti-mesenteric border for a corresponding length. A 3-0 PDS suture is used to create the posterior full thickness layer, starting from the left side and progressing towards the right side.
The surgeon takes suturing bites that include the gastric mucosa, ensuring the inclusion of the serosa of both the stomach and ileum to prevent retraction and secure the anastomosis.
The surgeon tightens the suture periodically during the anastomosis, which can alternatively be performed using a 45 mm tri-stapler.
The surgeon performs a stapled anastomosis, which can be oriented either horizontally or vertically, using a stapler to join the gastric sleeve and ileal loop.
The surgeon applies a knot to secure the posterior full thickness layer on the right side, then continues suturing the anterior full thickness layer from right to left using the same suture material.
The surgeon takes bites that include the gastric mucosa while suturing, ensuring the serosa of the stomach and ileum are incorporated, but avoids including vaginal mucosa.
The surgeon takes suturing bites beyond the blanched area on the serosa of the stomach and ileum, ensuring secure tissue approximation during the duodenal switch procedure.
The surgeon carefully takes suture bites while avoiding visible blood vessels, periodically tightening the suture to ensure a secure anastomosis.
The surgeon reaches the left end of the anastomosis and completes the inner layer by applying a knot to secure the suture.

The surgeon continues the posterior seromuscular layer as the anterior seromuscular layer, starting from the left side and progressing towards the right side, effectively burying the anterior inner layer.
The surgeon completes the outer layer of the anastomosis by applying a knot at the right end, finalizing the two-layer technique to reduce tension on the anastomosis.
The surgeon applies anchoring switches on either side of the anastomosis for additional security. The wide gap behind the anastomosis is left open, as the surgeon chooses not to close it.

The surgeon uses methylene blue solution to test the patency of the anastomosis. The gastric sleeve staple line and anastomosis are covered with surgical material, possibly surgical snow, for protection.
The surgeon places the removed stomach into a sterile plastic bag. The gastric sleeve created during the procedure has two visible outlets.
